examples.title: Gaussian Naïve Bayes Implementation
examples.description: Complete implementation of Gaussian NB with training and prediction functions
examples.explanation: Implementation shows: (1) Training calculates mean, variance, priors per class using MLE, (2) Gaussian PDF computes likelihood for each feature, (3) Prediction uses log-probabilities to avoid underflow, (4) Smoothing (1e-9) added to variance to prevent division by zero
examples.timeComplexity: Training: O(n×d), Prediction: O(d) per sample

examples.title: Gaussian NB Training Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for parameter estimation in Gaussian Naïve Bayes
examples.code:
FUNCTION Train_GaussianNB(X_train, y_train):
 INPUT: X_train (n×d matrix), y_train (n vector)
 OUTPUT: Model parameters (means, variances, priors)
 
 classes ← UNIQUE(y_train)
 FOR each class c IN classes:
 X_c ← FILTER(X_train, y_train == c)
 n_c ← ROW_COUNT(X_c)
 
 // MLE for mean: sample mean
 mean[c][j] ← (1/n_c) × Σ x_i[j] FOR j = 1 to d
 
 // MLE for variance: sample variance
 var[c][j] ← (1/n_c) × Σ (x_i[j] - mean[c][j])² + ε FOR j = 1 to d
 
 // Class prior
 prior[c] ← n_c / n_total
 
 RETURN (mean, var, prior)

FUNCTION Predict_GaussianNB(x_test, mean, var, prior):
 FOR each class c IN classes:
 log_posterior[c] ← log(prior[c])
 FOR each feature j IN 1 to d:
 // Gaussian PDF in log form
 log_likelihood[j] ← -0.5 × log(2π × var[c][j]) - (x_test[j] - mean[c][j])² / (2 × var[c][j])
 log_posterior[c] ← log_posterior[c] + log_likelihood[j]
 
 RETURN argmax_c log_posterior[c]
examples.explanation: Training uses MLE to estimate μ and σ². Classification computes log-posterior for each class by summing log-likelihoods (converts product to sum). The ε (smoothing) prevents zero variance problem.

examples.title: Gaussian NB Probability Density Calculation
examples.description: Manual calculation of Gaussian PDF and posterior probability computation
examples.explanation: Shows explicit Gaussian PDF calculation and posterior computation. Includes numerical stability tricks: (1) log-space computation, (2) small epsilon to prevent log(0), (3) log-sum-exp normalization to get proper probabilities from log-posteriors.
